text:
Sustainable hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂) production via photocatalytic water oxidation represents a promising alternative to the traditional industrial anthraquinone process, yet achieving high efficiency in complex media like seawater remains a significant challenge. In this work, we report the development of an atomically dispersed manganese single-atom photocatalyst (SAPC) anchored on aryl amino-substituted graphitic carbon nitride (g-C₃N₄). This material demonstrates exceptional performance, generating up to 2230 µM of H₂O₂ in alkaline pure water and 1800 µM in seawater after 7 hours of visible light irradiation, maintaining high stability and recyclability without deactivation by seawater salts.

Mechanistic investigations, supported by isotope labeling and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy, reveal that H₂O₂ is produced through a dual two-electron water oxidation reaction (WOR) pathway. Specifically, photogenerated holes facilitate both a direct one-step oxidation of H₂O to H₂O₂ and an indirect route involving the recombination of hydroxyl radicals (•OH). Advanced characterization—including X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XANES/EXAFS), high-resolution TEM, and solid-state NMR—confirms the existence of isolated Mn²⁺ sites in two distinct coordination environments. Multi-scale molecular modeling further elucidates that the aryl amino groups shift the valence band to a more positive potential and enhance visible light absorption, while the Mn centers promote charge separation and oxygen activation. This study provides a robust framework for designing non-precious-metal single-atom catalysts for solar-to-chemical energy conversion in saline environments.
